title: Close the Medigap Act of 2025
stage_label: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be
plain_summary: The Close the Medigap Act of 2025 makes it easier and fairer for people to buy Medigap insurance, which helps pay for medical costs that Medicare does not cover. The law says insurance companies must sell these policies to anyone who has Medicare, even if they have health problems. It also stops companies from charging much higher prices to older people and requires them to spend more of the money they collect on actual medical care instead of keeping it as profit. The bill also makes the Medicare website better so people can understand their insurance choices more easily.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Ways and Means, and in addition to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Close the Medigap Act of twenty twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Guaranteed Issue
sections.text: This section changes the rules for Medigap policies. Medigap policies are private insurance plans that help pay for costs that Medicare does not cover. Under the new rules, insurance companies that sell Medigap policies must follow these requirements for people who have Medicare Part A hospital insurance and Part B medical insurance. First, companies cannot refuse to sell a policy or charge more money because of a person's health problems, past medical claims, or medical conditions. Second, companies cannot exclude coverage for health problems that existed before someone bought the policy. Third, companies cannot make people wait before their coverage starts. Fourth, companies cannot refuse to sell or charge more for a policy based on a person's genetic information. Fifth, companies must sell any Medigap policy they offer to new customers. Sixth, companies cannot limit when people can sign up for coverage. These rules do not apply to people who only have Medicare Part A because they have kidney disease. Companies can still charge more or deny coverage to employers based on an employee's health condition, but they cannot use one person's genetic information to make decisions about other people in a group. The Secretary of Health and Human Services must create a plan to tell Medicare beneficiaries about these changes. The Secretary will work with consumer groups, insurance brokers, insurance companies, state insurance regulators, and state programs that help people with Medicare questions. These new rules start on January first twenty twenty six. The Secretary can phase in these changes gradually starting on that date to avoid problems for people who already have Medigap policies. However, all changes must be fully in place by January first twenty thirty one.

sections.title: Section 3 Medical Loss Ratio
sections.text: This section changes the rules about how much of the money from insurance premiums must be spent on actual medical care instead of company profits and overhead costs. The current rules require that at least seventy five percent of group policy premiums and at least sixty five percent of individual policy premiums go toward medical benefits. Starting January first twenty twenty six, insurance companies must meet both the current requirements and any higher percentage that the National Association of Insurance Commissioners recommends to the Secretary. The standards must also be reviewed regularly to make sure they stay appropriate.

sections.title: Section 4 Limitations on Pricing Discrimination
sections.text: This section requires the Secretary to ask the National Association of Insurance Commissioners to review and update the standards for all Medigap benefit packages. The new standards will prevent certain types of unfair pricing. Under these revised standards, insurance companies cannot charge different prices based on a person's age. Companies also cannot charge higher prices that would make it hard for people with disabilities or people with kidney disease to afford coverage just because they qualify for Medicare due to their condition. Additionally, companies must set the same premium prices for everyone living in the same county or area as designated by the Secretary. These new pricing rules will apply to Medigap policies sold to people who first become eligible for Medicare Part A or enroll in Medicare Part B starting January first twenty twenty six.

sections.title: Section 5 Clarifying Beneficiary Options on the Medicare Plan Finder Website
sections.text: This section requires improvements to the Medicare Plan Finder website that helps people compare their Medicare options. The website must provide information about doctor and hospital networks so people understand which providers they can use and what it might cost. The website must show out-of-pocket costs including deductibles, copayments, coinsurance, monthly premiums, and estimated yearly costs broken down by category. Until January first twenty twenty six, the website must explain the current rules in each state about when people can buy Medigap policies without health questions, and clearly explain what happens in states that do not guarantee people can buy these policies if they have health problems. Starting January first twenty twenty six and regularly after that, the Secretary must review the website to make sure it gives people the information they need to make good decisions about their Medicare coverage. Every year, the Secretary must ask the public for suggestions about how to improve the website's presentation of Medigap options. The website updates must make information easier to understand for the average person. The updates should help people compare premiums, understand policy differences, learn about insurance company financial stability, see how much premiums have increased over time, and understand the rules about guaranteed access to Medigap policies. The website must also explain costs for Medicare Advantage plans and prescription drug plans. Before making any changes to the website, the Secretary must test the information with consumers, share the test results with interested groups, and allow sixty days for public comments.

sections.title: Section 6 Restoring Access to First Dollar Medigap Coverage
sections.text: This section removes a restriction that previously prevented some Medigap policies from covering the very first dollar of medical expenses. This change allows insurance companies to offer more comprehensive coverage options that pay for costs from the first dollar spent on medical care.

sections.title: Section 7 Broker Transparency
sections.text: This section requires insurance companies that sell Medigap policies to report payments they make to insurance agents, brokers, and other people who sell their policies. Starting within twelve months of when this law passes, companies must submit yearly reports to the Secretary about money or other valuable things they gave to these salespeople during the previous year. The reports must include the name and business address of each person who received payments, the amount paid, the dates of payment, what form the payment took, and any other information the Secretary thinks is important. The same transparency rules that already apply to drug and medical device companies will also apply to these Medigap insurance reports.
